title: Fitting a window sill board
subtitle: Cut and fix an internal timber window board level on the masonry sill
excerpt: How to measure, cut, fit, and fix an internal timber window board (window sill). Covers marking the back edge to follow the wall line, planing a bevel where the board bears on the sloping masonry sill, cutting the horns to fit into the window reveal, and bedding and fixing the board.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The internal timber sill fixed across the bottom of a window opening. The window board projects past the wall face (the nosing) and sits on the masonry sill, which typically slopes forward at 5 to 10 degrees to shed water.

The short projection at each end of a window board that fits into the plaster reveal at each side of the window opening. The horn prevents the board end from being visible and gives the board a solid bearing on the wall plaster.

content.text: sits on the masonry sill and projects 20 to 30 mm (3/4 to 1 1/4 in) beyond the plaster face. The masonry sill slopes, so the board's back edge must be planed to a matching bevel or packed level. Most boards also need
cut to fit into the plaster reveal at each side.

Measure the width of the opening at the wall face, and the depth from the front of the window frame to the wall face plus the 20 to 30 mm (3/4 to 1 1/4 in) nosing projection. Measure the height of the sill at each side of the opening (the sill may slope front to back or side to side on an older building). Note the depth of the plaster reveal at each side so the horns can be marked to fit.
Step 2, cut the board to length and mark the horns

Cut the board to the full width including both horns (total width equals the opening width plus the depth of the reveal at each side). Mark the horn notch on each end: the horn depth equals the reveal depth and the horn length equals the reveal height. Cut the horn notches with a tenon saw, sawing across the grain first then ripping along the grain to remove the waste.
Step 3, plane the back edge bevel

Set a sliding bevel or combination square to the angle of the masonry sill and transfer this bevel to the back edge of the board. Plane the back edge to this angle with a block plane so the board sits flat on the sloping sill rather than rocking on its back corner. Test the board in position; it should sit flush on the sill without rocking and without a visible gap at the back edge.
Step 4, bed and fix the board

Run a bead of flexible adhesive (neutral-cure silicone or grab adhesive) along the masonry sill and at the back edge of the reveal notches. Press the board into position. Fix through the board into the timber window frame at the back edge using 50 mm No. 8 screws countersunk into the board face and filled. Do not rely on the adhesive alone; the sill must be mechanically fixed.

Apply a bead of flexible decorator's caulk along the joint between the back edge of the board and the wall plaster, and between the ends of the horns and the side reveals. Smooth with a wet finger. Do not use a rigid filler at these joints; thermal and moisture movement at a window opening cracks rigid fillers within the first year.

attrs.symptom: Board rocks on the masonry sill
attrs.cause: The back-edge bevel does not match the sill slope, so the board is bearing on a single edge rather than flat.
attrs.fix: Remove the board and plane the back edge to a steeper bevel until the board sits flat on the sill without rocking. Check with a try square along the top face of the board.

attrs.symptom: Gap appears between the back edge of the board and the wall after a few months
attrs.cause: The board has dried slightly and shrunk across its width, or the caulk joint has dried and cracked.
attrs.fix: Re-apply flexible decorator's caulk. Use a species with lower movement (MDF window board, or engineered timber) if the gap recurs annually.
